data.title: Macro Insights: Hot Takes And The Trade War's Resurgence
data.description:
Introduction

Welcome to the SA’s The Macro Brief! This official SA Profile will highlight our analysts' latest economic and market analysis to help investors gauge the ever-volatile financial landscape through various recurring series.

The economy is the foundation of financial markets, influencing everything from corporate earnings and consumer spending to central bank policy and inflation. Understanding economic trends, policy decisions, and sector activity can be critical when assessing market opportunities and making informed investment decisions.

Editor's Note: This is the first installment of the Macro Insights series, publishing semi-monthly.

Check out these must-reads from October’s first half…

Trending Themes

Is This A Hot Take?

Why I Think The S&P 500 Has Become Irrelevant, And What I'm Doing About It | Sungarden Investment Publishing | 10/5/25

“The S&P 500 Index Has Lost Most Of Its Relevance. It Has Essentially Been The Target Of A Not-So-Hostile Takeover By The Nasdaq 100 Index, Particularly The Giant Stocks At The Top.”

This Hasn't Happened Since 1966 And Proves We're Just Entering A Bull Market | Multiplo Invest | 10/6/25

“…we are witnessing the 4th strongest rally in history, but what most caught my attention in this study is that we are 116 days without a pullback of 6%. This only occurred in 1957 and 1966, when the market was only at the beginning of a large bull market.”

Will P/E Ratios Ever Get Back To Normal? | James A. Kostohryz | 10/8/25

“Current high PEs may persist for 5-10 years unless expectations for AI-driven growth or macroeconomic conditions change significantly.”

A Major Market Shift Could Be Coming | High Yield Investor | 10/9/25

“So what does this mean for me? It means that I am steering clear of SPY and the leading megacap tech stocks that are increasingly dominating the index in favor of blue-chip dividend growth stocks.”

Something Feels Off - And Billionaires Know It | Leo Nelissen | 10/11/25

“Market conditions resemble past bubbles, but AI-driven disruption and secular growth trends present significant long-term opportunities for prudent investors.”

Tariffs Come Screaming Back

Don't Be Fooled By The Tariff Bears Again | JR Research | 10/12/25

“Let's try and dissect what the market is so terribly concerned about right now? President Trump's 100% tariff threat on China should be construed as a move to "encourage" China to back off from its plans to clamp down on rare earth exports that could hamstring America's economy.”

One Does Not Simply Impose 100% Tariffs On China | Trapping Value | 10/13/25

“You thought you were done with the tariff wars. After the April selloff and the multiple rounds of negotiations, it seemed that this was a topic ready to be put to bed. Our last take on this was that the administration will push as hard as the equity markets will allow them to.”

US-China Trade War Escalates To Geopolitical Crisis | Damir Tokic | 10/14/25

“China is escalating not only the trade war but also the geopolitical situation by banning exports of rare earths for military use, which affects the US Army and NATO.”

Forget Tariffs - The Real Market Killer Is Already Here | James Foord | 10/15/25

“Stocks sold off sharply after renewed U.S.-China tariff fears, but this might just be the excuse markets were looking for.”

To Burst, Or Float Higher

Bubble Trouble: It's Like 2007 All Over Again | Hawkinvest | 10/3/25

“The U.S. stock market is hitting record highs despite mounting economic warning signs reminiscent of 2007, raising concerns of a potential correction.”

Don't Waste Your Time Timing Bubbles: Subscriptions Make This Market Nothing Like 1999 | Brett Ashcroft Green | 10/10/25

“It's fair to make a lot of comparisons to 1999; we're trading along the same CAPE ratio trends, price to sales, and price to cash flow, all of which have similarities. Earnings growth is quite a bit different, however, as is earnings resiliency.”

Conclusion

We hope our analysts' insights serve as a valuable financial compass for investors seeking to make informed investment decisions.

Thanks for reading. Tell us what you think by commenting below! If you'd like to see more, follow this account.

Please let us know if you have any suggestions you’d like to see added to this series and any ideas for new additional recurring series. We're always open to your valuable feedback.

data.title: MSCI Inc.: Its Weakening Moat Merits A Sell
data.description:
Thesis

MSCI Inc.'s (NYSE:MSCI) share price has returned to roughly the same level it traded at in 2021. With dividend yield of 1.3% it is hard to say it acted as a compounder. Momentum was bad. According to Seeking Alpha’s Momentum Grade, it now carries a D+. However, that alone might tempt investors to ask whether the company has finally become a buy. We asked ourselves the same question. What we found instead were many risks that the market seems determined to ignore.

In our view, MSCI represents one of the most fascinating cases of a company that became a standard and then a prisoner of that same standard. Its moat is real and wide. It still commands respect. Yet, we believe it has stopped expanding. The business now grows not through innovation but through a rising market.

The heavy reliance on AUM fees from a concentrated client base creates a structural risk. In our opinion, the relationship no longer strengthens the moat. As ETF platforms consolidate, bargaining power moves toward distributors and not benchmark suppliers. With leverage rising and diversification pillars showing fatigue, there are plenty of incentives from all sides — S&P Global Inc. (SPGI), Moody’s Corporation (MCO), and BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) among them — to strategically prepare a battle they intend to win, almost in the spirit of Sun Tzu, long before MSCI even realizes it has begun.

We believe the market is still pricing MSCI as if its moat were widening. Our work suggests the opposite. Reverse valuation points to double-digit growth assumptions. Mounting headwinds we expect are just not priced in.

In short, MSCI remains a great company. But it has become one fighting yesterday’s war with yesterday’s weapons. Our rating starts with SELL.

Moat

Moat of MSCI is often considered to be wide. We would agree, but we also think this is not the whole story. In our view, the moat is no longer expanding. It has become static. And the way siege equipment is being built has not stopped evolving.

Today, MSCI has become standard of modern investing. Its most recognized benchmarks are across investment mandates and regulatory frameworks. That level of integration once reflected superb product quality. Now, it reflects lock-in. Once a standard becomes a requirement, it ceases to compete and evolve.

We see a situation like this as both; a strength and a limitation. Due to regulation, the largest institutions already benchmark almost everything that can be benchmarked. Marginal buyer is not a new fund, it is a rising market. In our view, the relationship with BlackRock shows this problem in all its glory. According to earnings data, around half of MSCI’s asset-based fees, or approximately 10% of total company revenue, comes directly from the iShares relationship.

With ETF industry consolidation (here, and here), bargaining power moves to platforms such as iShares, which command investor flows. Not to the index providers who supply the benchmarks.

So, could BlackRock eventually just work without MSCI and walk away? We would say yes. Technically. BlackRock's Aladdin platform already has the data infrastructure, risk models and quantitative know-how to create new indices. In this case, we do not think that the obstacle is technical but a matter of market trust. We believe that regulators and investors continue to prefer third-party benchmarks, and the liquidity of iShares funds depends heavily on the recognition of the MSCI name.

In our view, changing that name would damage that perception of neutrality and could even introduce tracking errors. Credibility in finance means a lot. However, it is not a permanent advantage. As fee compression increases pressure across the ETF industry, the incentive to internalise benchmarks also rises. And in our view, BlackRock has a lot of incentives to do so. But, they do not need to abandon MSCI entirely. They only need to experiment with internal benchmarks in smaller or thematic products. Over time, such experiments provide leverage at renewal. Pricing power does not disappear with a bang. It fades slowly through negotiation. Here, Sun Tzu quote comes to mind.

Victorious warriors win first and then go to war, while defeated warriors go to war first and then seek to win.

Even if iShares renews the agreement in 2030, which we think it will, it is likely they will demand lower fees. Or maybe a multi-provider model. There are a lot of providers to choose from. S&P Dow Jones, FTSE Russell and Morningstar are waiting in line. In any case by our view, a lot of room to maneuver for Mr. Fink.

These companies already control large portions of global ETF assets. S&P Dow Jones Indices is champion in the market with roughly 1/3 of global index share and remains deeply integrated into CME futures and U.S. equity benchmarks. FTSE Russell follows with about 11%, supported by a broad range of multi-asset and fixed income indices. FTSE may lack the prestige of MSCI, but it benefits from distribution and diversification from its connection with LSE. MSCI sells exposure, while FTSE sells infrastructure. With around 9% market share, MSCI is a strong but not dominant third player.

So, it is not just BlackRock or the distribution side of the business environment where we see that the company is poorly positioned for the next battle. Credit rating agencies such as S&P and Moody’s compete with MSCI across several segments. And they also have incentives to tighten the screws. And they did just that, but more on that under the financials part of this article. So, while their core franchises are rooted in credit ratings, both have expanded aggressively into areas like data services, analytics and benchmarking. MSCI just gave them another leverage to push.

From a strategic standpoint, we believe MSCI has positioned itself poorly. Heavy reliance on leveraged buybacks and maintaining valuation multiples above fundamentals has left its balance sheet vulnerable to a downgrade. A weaker credit rating would immediately raise funding costs and reduce flexibility to defend its moat. In our view, once MSCI’s perceived stability comes into question, the illusion of benchmark neutrality begins to erode. And when neutrality erodes, liquidity follows.

There are other challengers, such as Bloomberg Indices, STOXX and Nasdaq, that continue to take small pieces of market share. And those smaller challengers are where true risk lies. They have the technology, know-how and incentive to grow and to take part of MSCI business. With much better pricing for BlackRock, every new mandate outside the MSCI slightly reduces its network effect and pricing power. The cost of switching remains significant, but the cost of threatening to switch is almost nothing. When the client controls trillions of dollars, that threat carries weight. A lot of weight.

Meanwhile, the company’s diversification pillars, including Analytics, ESG and Private Assets, are showing visible signs of ageing.

In Analytics, Barra and RiskMetrics once were represented as the gold standard of risk analysis. New competitors such as Axioma by SimpCorp A/S (privately held) and the aforementioned Aladdin 8 (by BlackRock) operate with modular and open architectures. They can integrate seamlessly into institutional data systems. The difference is not only technical but architectural since new challengers have been built as SaaS from scratch.

ESG data, once a buzzword and a long-term growth engine, has become politically charged in the United States and increasingly regulated in Europe.

Moat expansion or moat shrinkage?

As we have seen from history, moat can widen and shrink. Management is often forced to choose a new direction for the company when things get to a standstill. MSCI management describes its new direction as “customisation at scale.” We believe it is better described as a search for oxygen after the core franchise has reached maturity. MSCI already dominates the equity benchmark market. There is little left to win.

The company’s recent initiatives, including custom indices, private-market data and wealth analytics, sound ambitious but appear more defensive than transformative in our view.

At the September 2025 Virtual Open House, management reported 15 thousand custom indices tied to $1 trillion in assets. Data shows that this part of the business, MSCI has been growing with CAGR of more than 20% since 2022. Much of this new business comes from custom or white-label products like active ETFs, structured notes, and direct indexing strategies where the economics are thinner by design and the MSCI brand sits quietly in the background. We would argue that "customization at scale" may sound like innovation, but in practice it risks turning proprietary IP into a commodity service. In these areas, the MSCI brand is often secondary, and pricing power is weak.

For us, the same logic applies to the artificial intelligence narrative. Integration of the Foxberry engine improves internal efficiency and allows faster creation of indices, but it also lowers the barrier for imitation. What once required high expertise can now be replicated in hours. Automation may increase speed, but it also accelerates commoditisation. AI can bring a level playing field for all. That high friction that once protected the moat is disappearing. It is like new, cheap drones vs old expensive tanks in the unfortunate wars we have seen.

Then, there is a partnership with Moody’s and the expansion into private assets. It tells a similar story in our view. Burgiss and Real Capital Analytics are presented as a long-term investment in private-market standardisation. Yet, the reality and our experience is that the space is already dominated by Preqin Ltd., PitchBook Data, Inc., and other established players. A single proprietary dataset, no matter how detailed, cannot create the same network effect that made MSCI’s equity indices dominant.

In our view, the Wealth Manager initiative sounds appealing in theory but operates in a different economic landscape. The market for wealth data and analytics is already crowded, led by Morningstar by Morningstar, Inc. (MORN), FactSet by FactSet Research Systems Inc. (FDS) and Envestnet by Envestnet, Inc. (ENV). Margins are lower, and customization does not scale efficiently.

In our opinion, MSCI now resembles a utility. We think it is still essential. It is trusted and difficult to replace, but its pricing is increasingly dictated by the very clients and structures it helped to build. There are a lot of incentives to bring the moat down from the ones who helped to create it. The company still defines the standard. What remains uncertain is whether the standard will continue to define the growth.

Financials

Over the years, MSCI has been highly active in repurchasing its own shares. We welcome buybacks when a stock is genuinely undervalued and when there are no better options to make capital work. But that is hardly the case here. The company’s average P/E over the past five years has hovered around x50. According to SA valuation factor, the stock carries an “F” rating. At current levels, the buybacks look less like a return of value to shareholders and more like value destruction disguised as discipline.

What makes this even more concerning is the growing debt load combined with a negative equity position. While this for sure is not an immediate solvency risk, it amplifies strategic vulnerability. By leveraging for buybacks, MSCI gave its rivals additional leverage. When the licensing agreement comes up for renewal, that imbalance could become a bargaining weapon.

In our view, the moat has been weakened in pursuit of short-term optics. And when you publicly show where you are weak, you can be certain that an “enemy” will strike exactly there.

And, some cracks can be seen. The company's credit rating by its competitors is BBB-. Talk about a crack in a moat. Siege equipment truly keeps on evolving.

As of mid-2025 it carried $4.5 billion in debt and $300 million in cash translating to 2.3x net debt to EBITDA. In August 2025 the firm issued $1.25 billion in new debt taking advantage of a favorable market window and bringing leverage back toward range of 3–3.5x EBITDA. Most proceeds were used for share repurchases consistent with management’s view. With maturities spread between 2029 and 2035, 2030 BlackRock renewal contract comes to mind.

To assess credit rating, we use the net interest coverage ratio with data provided by Professor Damodaran from NYU Stern. Most sources use formula for interest coverage ratio as EBIT divided by interest cost. We think that it is fair toward the company to use net interest cost due to the current interest rate environment. With EBIT of $1.607 million and net interest cost of $169 million, we calculate an interest coverage ratio of x9.5.

And this detail shows a warning rating agencies are flashing to investors, or one can take it as a war strategy by competitors. Although, with interest coverage ratio of 9.5x MSCI should be in AAA rating due to current financials, it is in a much lower grade due to situation stated. If any sign of prolonged market stress appears, BlackRock negotiates the deal, MSCI could be the one exposed of swimming naked. High current margins hide the risk that the operating leverage of an AUM-based business can go both ways.

Reverse Valuation

It can get emotionally quite hard to give SELL recommendation to a company with wide moat, high margins and growing earnings. But, we feel that all stated risks are not included fully in price and that there is an asymetrical risk-reward profile for MSCI.

First, let us start with history just to have some baseline.

MSCI grew earnings in 2019-2024 period with CAGR of 14.5%. Although CapEx was small, the company was busy with the M&A part of the business, with $288 million average M&A. FCFE grew 15.6% in stated period.

Please note that we ignore all changes in working capital and debt levels. This is to focus on the core business, and due to our opinion that changes in working capital and debt levels shouldn't have a meaningful impact on valuation levels.

The reverse valuation model attempts to identify assumptions that lead to the current price. We are using the FCFE model.

The price at the moment of writing this article is around $550, the 10-year treasury yield is 4% and 5-year beta is 1.17.

Market consensus for FY 2025 is EPS of $17.06 and with 77.4 million shares we get consensus earnings of $1.320 billion. We project D&A and CapEx to grow in line with earnings and M&A to be in average level stated. This gives us ratio of D&A at 48% vs historical 28% in D&A+CapEx+M&A. This is something that checks out with priced in lower growth rate of 13.8% in period of 2026-2029 vs historical stated of 14.5%. For the next 10 years the company would need to grow at a "slower" rate of 11.04% going to RFR at perpetuity. Our logic in using a perpetual growth rate equal to the RFR is to mitigate large changes in share price due to slight changes in the RFR.

So, although a modest slowdown is priced in, strong headwinds from factors stated in this article are not factored in at all.

Risks to the Bearish Thesis

In our view, there are a couple of things that could challenge or at least delay the bearish scenario we outlined above.

MSCI’s moat is not easily undone. Despite growing competition, the trust in its brand and regulatory alignment gives it time that can last longer than rational expectations. The cost of switching benchmarks still is meaningful. It is not necessarily a structural defence, but one that can make bearish timing wrong even if the direction is right.

Then there is ability of management to repackage legacy assets into new stories should not be underestimated. “Customization at scale,” while defensive in our view, may succeed commercially. In an environment where story is often more important than fundamentals, this could make valuation multiples on higher levels longer than fundamentals alone would justify.

The ETF industry remains on a secular growth path. Rising global adoption of passive investing, particularly in EM and wealth channels, could lift all boats. Even modest AUM growth in indexed products would expand asset-based fees and offset some of the pricing risks.

High margins and asset-light model can provide room for financial maneuver. With an interest coverage ratio near 9.5× and solid recurring cash flows, MSCI retains enough flexibility to refinance or re-lever if needed.

Finally, we know that AI can go both ways. The same AI and automation trends that erode barriers to entry can also improve MSCI’s internal productivity and scalability if done better than peers.

To sum up our bearish thesis risks. In markets, being early is often indistinguishable from being wrong.

Conclusion - SELL

We believe MSCI is a good company that remains trapped in an exceptional illusion. The brand still inspires trust, but the economics behind it are slowly eroding. What once was a fortress built on standardization has become a monument to it. The moat is still there, but it no longer widens. It defends what once was instead of shaping for what comes next.

Reverse valuation shows a lot of growth is priced in. A 50× multiple on maturing growth, increasing leverage, and rising client concentration risk leaves little margin for error. “Customization at scale” feels more like financial cosmetics than strategic renewal. We see a company that has monetized its past faster than it is building its future.

As we stated in the first chapters of the article, the moat is standing still while siege equipment is evolving.

Our conclusion is SELL.

data.title: Sempra: Strengthening Backbone Of U.S. Energy Infrastructure With $10 Billion LNG Infrastructure Deal
data.description:
Introduction

Since I first covered Sempra (NYSE:SRE) back in July and called them a “Key Player In A Vital Industry That's Potentially Undervalued,” the stock is up about 25%, with several developments both internally and in the macro environment.

However, some of these developments seem very important for the company’s future, so let’s go through them and update the thesis.

Internal Developments

To summarize their H1, Sempra’s first half of 2025 was solid overall, marked by very significant investments as part of their investment cycle, with plans to invest $13 billion this year with priority on their utility investments, while also planning to sell non-core assets in Mexico to simplify the business and reduce the portfolio’s risk. They’re also executing on their Fit for 2025 initiative to reduce costs, in an effort to save $300 million by phasing out some non-economic regulatory programs in California.

However, probably the biggest thing that happened for them was the sale of 45% ownership stake in Sempra Infrastructure for $10 billion ($11.61 billion or $10.93 billion gross when including the interest income; mostly expected in 2026 and 2027). Following the deal, Sempra will own 25% of Sempra Infrastructure.

As they say, this is expected to do several things for the company. Not only does it allow them to focus more on US utility growth plans, eliminate their planned equity needs in 2025 – 2029 capital plan and significantly strengthen their balance sheet, but they’re also projecting $0.20 EPS accretion on a full-year basis from 2027.

This transaction seems to be quite good for SRE overall, with the company affirming their FY2025 and 2026 guidance of $4.3 to $4.7 and $4.8 to $5.3, respectively. Plus, they reaffirmed their guidance at the high-end or even above the projected EPS CAGR of 7% to 9% for 2025 through 2029, which definitely played a key role in the stock’s performance.

Sempra’s Port Arthur Phase 2 was also part of a 20-year agreement with ConocoPhillips (COP), where the latter agreed to purchase 4 million metric tons per year of LNG, in addition to their 5 million metric tons agreement from Port Arthur’s Phase 1 (expected to be operational in 2027), with COP being the largest single buyer of the facility. That’s a total of 9 mmt/year out of a total capacity of ~26 mmt/year once Phase 2 is also online. And that's on top of an agreement for 2 mmt/year with EQT (EQT) announced shortly after. The project also secured a $7 billion investment from Blackstone (BX), representing significant funding and allowing it to advance.

However, this deal also massively lowers the company’s exposure to the Port Arthur, ECA and Cameron LNG projects, which may not be the best idea given the importance of these projects, but it’s true that they are unlocking a lot of value that would’ve been tied to a relatively uncertain future since it was based on commodity prices. Still, Sempra is now exposed to less business risk, and we could see it as a project that was developed at a time when the market was desperate for LNG and now capitalized on it, as they say:

The transactions announced today further Sempra’s corporate strategy by advancing the company’s capital recycling program and transition to a leading U.S. utility growth business

Still, as we can see, their Texas and California utility projects remain very significant and are expected to keep benefiting from scale advantages, expecting the project rate base to grow from $57 billion in 2027 to $80 billion in 2029.

Their Q3 Earnings Report is scheduled on November 5, with EPS expected to reach $0.92 and about $2.98 billion in revenue. I am really looking forward to several things in this report, especially their view on the current macroenvironment given the significant developments, as well as more nuance into their expectations and plans. Also, I believe it’s worth mentioning that now that they’re getting such a massive influx of capital, it wouldn’t be impossible to see them go into some acquisitions, and I think it could be a good idea to diversify their utility segment through more geopolitical exposure.

Macro Developments

On the macro side, rate cuts were probably the most important thing that happened, with two more rate cuts expected this year after the one in September. These could offer a lot of breathing room for the economy, as they could lower the cost of debt and increase discretionary budgets. This can then result in an even more ramped-up investment cycle into AI/EV/Cloud/etc. from companies, on top of Big Tech already investing totals of hundreds of billions this year alone, which can increase the need for Sempra’s services and support their potential expansion and favorable commodity prices.

Still, gold reached $4,200 per ounce recently, after breaking several records this year, and that says something very clear about the market’s expectations around the US economy, the dollar and global markets. This type of environment can lead to more pressure on the stock market and also on SRE, as the company depends significantly on the US economy after all.

Also important especially for Sempra Infrastructure could be an end to the war in Ukraine in the future, on top of the early steps towards peace in the Israel-Palestine war, for now. Remember that Port Arthur and basically most of the “Third Wave” of LNG were significantly influenced by the war, as it caused very significant shifts in the global LNG market, so its end could also revert some of these beneficial effects for the market.

But sure, the long-term demand for energy given the massive investments we see now in data centers, cloud, EVs, etc. should also be very significant. Right now, we are looking at a ton of uncertainty in the market due to Trump’s new tariff threats on China, retaliation from them, rising trade war fear, etc. on top of fear around their effect on the economy especially now when paired with rate cuts, but I believe the long-term should remain very solid for Sempra, as the industry’s tailwinds combined with their significant expansion projects ($56 billion plan based on their latest upsizing) remain very solid.

And of course, that comes on top of their geopolitical risks, including the exposure to California. I say that because we’ve already seen California cutting the amount requested by Sempra’s SoCalGas in their General Rate Case, approving a revenue requirement of $3.806 billion for SoCalGas, meaning ~$629 million (~14.2%) less than Sempra sought, which 9.3% lower compared to 2023, as I said in the initial analysis where I detail this process a bit more.

Valuation

Using the midpoint EPS of $4.5 in 2025 and $5.05 in 2026, we get to earnings of $2.94 billion in 2025 and $3.3 billion in 2026, and assuming an 8% CAGR through 2029 (they expect 7% to 9% or even above following the Infrastructure deal), followed by a 5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years given the industry’s potential given the significant AI/EV tailwinds, as well as a 3% terminal growth rate. I’ll go for a 6.5% WACC (vs my estimated ~6.26% based on a cost of equity of 8.5% and ~3.5% cost of debt), as well as $36.3 billion in net debt and 653.22 million shares outstanding based on their latest report, even though this will look slightly different after the deal is complete. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $112.37 billion, and after subtracting the net debt, that’s an equity value of $76.07 billion, meaning about $116.45 per share.

Conclusions

Overall, Sempra remains a Buy for me given their very important strategic assets, growth potential and financials on top of the industry’s tailwinds, while also offering a decent dividend on top. Of course, given their dependency on the economy and commodities, that doesn’t mean they’re the safest investment out there, but it seems like a solid pick especially for a retirement portfolio and pension/other funds.
